findings.title: Ransomware Actors Exploit Unpatched SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management to Compromise Utility Billing Software Provider
findings.summary: Summary The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) is releasing this advisory in response to ransomware actors leveraging unpatched instances of a vulnerability in SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) to compromise customers of a utility billing software provider. This incident reflects a broader pattern of ransomware actors targeting organizations through unpatched versions of SimpleHelp RMM since January 2025. SimpleHelp versions 5.5.7 and earlier contain several vulnerabilities, including CVE-2024-57727 —a path traversal vulnerability. 1 Ransomware actors likely leveraged CVE-2024-57727 to access downstream customers’ unpatched SimpleHelp RMM for disruption of services in double extortion compromises. 1 CISA added CVE-2024-57727 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) Catalog on Feb. 13, 2025. CISA urges software vendors, downstream customers, and end users to immediately implement the Mitigations listed in this advisory based on confirmed compromise or risk of compromise. Download the PDF version of this report: AA25-163A Ransomware Actors Exploit Unpatched SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management to Compromise Utility Billing Software Provider (PDF, 420.49 KB ) Mitigations CISA recommends organizations implement the mitigations below to respond to emerging ransomware activity exploiting SimpleHelp software. These mitigations align with the Cross-Sector Cybersecurity Performance Goals (CPGs) developed by CISA and the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST). The CPGs provide a minimum set of practices and protections that CISA and NIST recommend all organizations implement. CISA and NIST based the CPGs on existing cybersecurity frameworks and guidance to protect against the most common and impactful threats, tactics, techniques, and procedures. Visit CISA’s CPGs webpage for more information on the CPGs, including additional recommended baseline protections. These mitigations apply to all critical infrastructure

enrichment.technical_details: CVE-2024-57727 is an unauthenticated directory/path traversal (CWE-22) in the SimpleHelp RMM server. Crafted HTTP requests allow an unauthenticated remote attacker to read arbitrary files, including serverconfig.xml, which contains hashed administrator passwords and other secrets. The vulnerability is typically chained in a three-step attack: (1) CVE-2024-57727 path traversal is used to exfiltrate the server configuration and admin password hash; (2) the hash is cracked, or CVE-2024-57726 is used to abuse technician credentials, to obtain administrative access; and (3) CVE-2024-57728 is leveraged to achieve arbitrary code execution on the SimpleHelp server. Attackers then push unsolicited SimpleHelp technician client installations to downstream MSP customers for lateral movement and ransomware deployment.
enrichment.affected_products: SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) versions 5.5.7 and all earlier releases (including 5.5.0-5.5.7, 5.4.x and earlier, 5.3.x and earlier). Related vulnerabilities CVE-2024-57726 (improper access control) and CVE-2024-57728 (server-side code execution) are commonly chained with CVE-2024-57727.
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes - public proof-of-concept exploit code is available. A Python PoC targeting the path traversal is published at https://github.com/imjdl/CVE-2024-57727. Additional analysis and walkthroughs are publicly available (e.g., https://medium.com/@unKnOwn37/simplehelp-cve-2024-57727-tryhackme-3bea119c1c1e and https://www.offsec.com/blog/cve-2024-57727/).
enrichment.patch_available: Yes. SimpleHelp released patches on approximately January 8-13, 2025, ahead of public disclosure. Fixed versions: SimpleHelp 5.5.8 (and any later 5.5.x), plus backported fixes 5.4.10 and 5.3.9 on older supported branches. Patch information is documented at https://guides.simple-help.com/kb---security-vulnerabilities-01-2025 and https://simple-help.com/blogs/security-vulnerabilities-in-simplehelp-5-5-7-and-earlier-what-you-need-to-know.
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes - confirmed active exploitation in the wild. CISA added this CVE to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) Catalog on February 13, 2025, and published Advisory AA25-163A on June 12, 2025 documenting ransomware actors exploiting unpatched SimpleHelp RMM instances since January 2025 to compromise a utility billing software provider and its downstream customers in a double-extortion ransomware attack (DragonForce). Independent reporting includes Arctic Wolf (January 22, 2025), The Hacker News (February 7, 2025), Help Net Security (May 28, 2025), and Sophos (May 27, 2025).
enrichment.threat_actors: DragonForce ransomware actors (per CISA Advisory AA25-163A). CISA also documents a broader pattern of unnamed ransomware actors targeting unpatched SimpleHelp RMM instances since January 2025, including a campaign observed by Arctic Wolf on January 22, 2025 that leveraged SimpleHelp RMM for initial access and follow-on ransomware deployment.
enrichment.mitigation: Per CISA Advisory AA25-163A and SimpleHelp: (1) Immediately upgrade SimpleHelp servers to version 5.5.8 or later (or 5.4.10 / 5.3.9 on older branches). (2) For unpatched instances, isolate the SimpleHelp server from the internet or stop the SimpleHelp server process. (3) Change the SimpleHelp Administrator password and all Technician account passwords (unless authenticated via SSO/AD), and rotate any API tokens. (4) Restrict at the firewall which IP addresses may reach the SimpleHelp server, which may log in as Technician/Administrator, and which may call APIs. (5) Create Server Event alerts for Administrator logins, failed login attempts, and configuration changes. (6) Audit Remote Access Services for unrecognized server URLs. (7) Threat-hunt for suspicious executables with three-alphabetic-letter filenames (e.g., aaa.exe, bbb.exe) created after January 2025. (8) Maintain offline/clean backups and do not expose services such as RDP directly to the internet.

findings.title: Unpatched Flaws Disclosed in Filesystem Bundled Into Millions of Embedded Devices
findings.summary:
Security firm runZero has disclosed seven vulnerabilities in FatFs, a small filesystem library that lets a device read and write the FAT and exFAT formats used on USB drives and SD cards.

The flaws matter because FatFs is nearly everywhere. It ships inside the firmware that runs security cameras, drones, industrial controllers, hardware crypto wallets, and other devices built on

enrichment.technical_details: Seven CVEs in ChaN's FatFs R0.16 and earlier: CVE-2026-6682 (CWE-190 integer overflow in FAT32 mount_volume allowing memory corruption / RCE) at 7.6; CVE-2026-6687 (CWE-121 stack-based buffer overflow in f_getlabel via uncapped exFAT label length) at 7.6; CVE-2026-6688 (CWE-120/CWE-121 — long filename overflow where up to 255-byte fno.fname values are copied via strcpy/sprintf into fixed-size caller buffers) at 7.6; CVE-2026-6685 (CWE-191 unsigned-subtraction wraparound in dirty-cache/fragmented-volume handling producing OOB effects and silent data corruption) at 6.1; CVE-2026-6683 (CWE-369 divide-by-zero in exFAT sync/write paths when crafted metadata produces n_fatent-2 == 0) at 4.6; CVE-2026-6686 (CWE-908 information disclosure on seeking beyond EOF leaks uninitialized cluster data) at 4.6; CVE-2026-6684 (CWE-835 unbounded loop in GPT partition scanning when a crafted GPT header sets GPTH_PtNum to an extreme value, hanging mount/boot) at 4.6. Attack vector requires physical access — a malicious removable-media (USB/SD) image is inserted or delivered via an OTA update channel that auto-mounts the image — to corrupt memory, brick the device, leak data, or achieve code execution on the target device.
enrichment.affected_products: FatFs R0.16 and earlier (all releases since R0.13), including vendored copies in Espressif ESP-IDF, STMicroelectronics STM32Cube middleware, Zephyr RTOS, MicroPython, ArduPilot, RT-Thread, Arm Mbed (legacy Keil), Samsung TizenRT, and SWUpdate; affected device classes include security cameras, drones, industrial controllers, hardware crypto wallets, ATMs, voting machines, and consumer IoT devices with USB/SD card interfaces
enrichment.cvss_vector: CVSS:3.1/AV:P/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H (highest-severity vector — applies to CVE-2026-6682 and CVE-2026-6687; other CVEs have related but lower-severity AV:P variants as listed in their NVD entries)
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes — public proof-of-concept available at https://github.com/runZeroInc/vulns-2026-fatfs-chance (crafted FAT/exFAT/GPT images plus a QEMU harness delivering reproducible code execution on bare-metal Cortex-M); NVD entries show PoC exploitation status per CISA SSVC.
enrichment.patch_available: No official upstream patch for six of seven CVEs. The FatFs upstream maintainer (ChaN at elm-chan.org) did not respond to coordinated disclosure. CVE-2026-6684 is fixed in the publicly released FatFs R0.16 (new test_gpt_header() validating GPT CRC32 and bounding PtNum to ≤128); the six remaining memory-corruption / information-disclosure CVEs (CVE-2026-6682, -6683, -6685, -6686, -6687, -6688) remain unpatched upstream. Downstream RTOS/SDK/OEM integrators must patch locally — fixes are expected to take years. References: https://www.runzero.com/blog/fatfs-bugs/ and https://www.runzero.com/advisories/fatfs-gpt-scan-loop-dos-cve-2026-6684/.
enrichment.active_exploitation: No confirmed in-the-wild exploitation as of the 2026-07-03 disclosure. runZero's research blog (https://www.runzero.com/blog/fatfs-bugs/) and adjacent reporting (The Hacker News, Risky Bulletin, CybersecurityNews) describe the flaws as discovered via AI-assisted research and reproduced in a controlled QEMU-based bare-metal test environment; no attacks against real-world deployments have been reported.
enrichment.mitigation: No upstream patches exist for six of seven CVEs. Only CVE-2026-6684 is fixed in FatFs R0.16 (new test_gpt_header() that validates GPT CRC32 and bounds PtNum to ≤128). Recommended hardening: (1) restrict or monitor physical access to USB/SD/removable-media ports on deployed devices; (2) restrict and validate OTA / firmware-update image sources; (3) audit vendored FatFs copies and especially downstream caller code that consumes fno.fname / file-size fields (strcpy/sprintf of LFN values); (4) actively track and apply downstream firmware updates as RTOS/SDK vendors and OEMs patch the library locally.
enrichment.vendor_advisory: https://www.runzero.com/blog/fatfs-bugs/ (and per-CVE pages under https://www.runzero.com/advisories/, e.g. https://www.runzero.com/advisories/fatfs-gpt-scan-loop-dos-cve-2026-6684/; the upstream vendor ChaN / elm-chan.org has NOT published an official advisory and did not respond to disclosure attempts)

findings.title: AI threats in the wild: The current state of prompt injections on the web
findings.summary: Posted by Thomas Brunner, Yu-Han Liu, Moni Pande At Google, our Threat Intelligence teams are dedicated to staying ahead of real-world adversarial activity, proactively monitoring emerging threats before they can impact users. Right now, Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) is a top priority for the security community, anticipating it as a primary attack vector for adversaries to target and compromise AI agents. But while the danger of IPI is widely discussed, are threat actors actually exploiting this vector today – and if so, how? To answer these questions and to uncover real-world abuse, we initiated a broad sweep of the public web to monitor for known indirect prompt injection patterns. This is what we found. The threat of indirect prompt injection Unlike a direct injection where a user "jailbreaks" a chatbot, IPI occurs when an AI system processes content—like a website, email, or document—that contains malicious instructions. When the AI reads this poisoned content, it may silently follow the attacker's commands instead of the user's original intent. This is not a new area of concern for us and Google has been working tirelessly to combat these threats. Our efforts involve cross-functional collaboration between researchers at Google DeepMind (GDM) and defenders like the Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG). We have previously detailed our work in this area and researchers have further highlighted the evolving nature of these vulnerabilities. Despite this collective focus, a fundamental question remains: to what degree are real-world malicious actors currently operationalizing these attacks? Proactive monitoring at Google The landscape of IPI on the web There are many channels through which attackers might try to send prompt injections. However, one location is particularly easy to observe - the public web. Here, threat actors may simply seed prompt injections on websites in hope of corrupting AI systems that browse them. Public research confirms these attacks ar

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) — OWASP LLM01:2025 — occurs when an LLM or AI agent ingests external, attacker-controlled content (web pages, search results, documents, email bodies/subjects, calendar event descriptions, code, file uploads) and treats embedded natural-language instructions as authoritative system-level commands, hijacking the model's behavior. Google's blog categorizes six observed in-the-wild intents: (1) harmless pranks (tone changes), (2) helpful-guidance manipulation, (3) SEO manipulation, (4) deterring/anti-agent abuse (pages that exhaust AI crawlers), (5) malicious data exfiltration, and (6) malicious destruction (commands such as 'delete all files'). A key subclass is GeminiJack, a zero-click RAG-poisoning attack against Gemini Enterprise/Vertex AI Search where a single shared payload in a Doc title, Calendar invite, or email subject silently triggers exfiltration of Gmail/Calendar/Docs data via tool calls.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Gemini (consumer), Google Gemini Workspace integration, Google Gemini Enterprise, Google Vertex AI Search, AI-powered browsing assistants, AI ad-review systems, and agentic LLM search/coding pipelines that ingest external content (web pages, Gmail, Google Docs, Google Calendar events).
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes — multiple public PoCs and observed in-the-wild payloads are available: GeminiJack zero-click PoC against Google Gemini Enterprise (Noma Labs, Dec 2025, https://noma.security/blog/geminijack-google-gemini-zero-click-vulnerability/); Atlas AI browser prompt-injection PoC (GitHub: brennanbrown/atlas-prompt-injection-poc); PayloadsAllTheThingsPrompt-Injection repository (https://swisskyrepo.github.io/PayloadsAllTheThings/Prompt%20Injection/); Forcepoint X-Labs '10 Indirect Prompt Injection Payloads Caught in the Wild' (Apr 22, 2026); Miggo 'Weaponizing Calendar Invites' against Gemini (Jan 19, 2026); Unit 42 observed 22 in-the-wild payload engineering techniques.
enrichment.patch_available: No single CVE-style patch exists because IPI is a vulnerability class, not a single bug. Google has shipped layered defenses across Gemini / Gemini Enterprise / Workspace (adversarial training in Gemini 2.5, content classifiers, markdown sanitization, Safe Browsing, user-confirmation framework) and continues to monitor and remove malicious content. The GeminiJack zero-click flaw in Gemini Enterprise was reported to Google on May 6, 2025 and mitigated prior to public disclosure on Dec 8, 2025. References: https://security.googleblog.com/2025/06/mitigating-prompt-injection-attacks.html and https://security.googleblog.com/2026/04/google-workspaces-continuous-approach-to-mitigating-indirect-prompt-injections.
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes — confirmed in-the-wild exploitation. The Google Security Blog (Apr 23, 2026) reports a 32% relative increase in malicious-category Indirect Prompt Injection attempts between November 2025 and February 2026 and explicitly states 'Our results indicate that attackers are experimenting with IPI on the web.' Observed malicious intents include data exfiltration and destruction (commands to wipe the user's machine). Independent corroboration: Palo Alto Unit 42 catalogued 22 distinct payload engineering techniques targeting AI ad-review systems and web-browsing agents; Forcepoint X-Labs published '10 Indirect Prompt Injection Payloads Caught in the Wild' (Apr 22, 2026); Miggo disclosed weaponized Calendar invites against Google Gemini (Jan 19, 2026); Noma Labs publicly disclosed GeminiJack in Google Gemini Enterprise (Dec 8, 2025).
enrichment.threat_actors: None known. The Google Security Blog (Apr 23, 2026) and corroborating research from Palo Alto Unit 42 characterize attackers only as generic adversaries experimenting with Indirect Prompt Injection; no named APT groups, campaigns, or ransomware operators are attributed.
enrichment.mitigation: Google's layered defense strategy includes: (1) Foundational model hardening via adversarial training data baked into Gemini 2.5; (2) Prompt-injection content classifiers scanning external content; (3) Security thought reinforcement instructing the model to prioritize user tasks; (4) Markdown sanitization and suspicious-URL redaction (Google Safe Browsing); (5) User-confirmation framework (human-in-the-loop) for sensitive operations; (6) End-user security mitigation notifications; (7) Continuous red-team pressure-testing and the AI Vulnerability Reward Program. Additional guidance for Workspace admins at https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/security/indirect-prompt-injections-and-googles-layered-defense-strategy-for-gemini; technical deep dive in 'Lessons from Defending Gemini Against Indirect Prompt Injections' (Google DeepMind security paper); practitioners can also follow the OWASP LLM Prompt Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet.

findings.title: Google Workspace’s continuous approach to mitigating indirect prompt injections
findings.summary: Posted by Adam Gavish, Google GenAI Security Team Indirect prompt injection (IPI) is an evolving threat vector targeting users of complex AI applications with multiple data sources, such as Workspace with Gemini. This technique enables the attacker to influence the behavior of an LLM by injecting malicious instructions into the data or tools used by the LLM as it completes the user’s query. This may even be possible without any input directly from the user. IPI is not the kind of technical problem you “solve” and move on. Sophisticated LLMs with increasing use of agentic automation combined with a wide range of content create an ultra-dynamic and evolving playground for adversarial attacks. That’s why Google takes a sophisticated and comprehensive approach to these attacks. We’re continuously improving LLM resistance to IPI attacks and launching AI application capabilities with ever-improving defenses. Staying ahead of the latest indirect prompt injection attacks is critical to our mission of securing Workspace with Gemini. In our previous blog “ Mitigating prompt injection attacks with a layered defense strategy ”, we reviewed the layered architecture of our IPI defenses. In this blog, we’ll share more detail on the continuous approach we take to improve these defenses and to solve for new attacks. New attack discovery By proactively discovering and cataloging new attack vectors through internal and external programs, we can identify vulnerabilities and deploy robust defenses ahead of adversarial activity. Human Red-Teaming Human Red-Teaming uses adversarial simulations to uncover security and safety vulnerabilities. Specialized teams execute attacks based on realistic user profiles to exploit weaknesses, coordinating with product teams to resolve identified issues. Automated Red-Teaming Automated Red-Teaming is done via dynamic, machine-learning-driven frameworks to stress-test environments. By algorithmically generating and iterating on attack payloads, we can mi

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect prompt injection (IPI) is an attacker technique against LLM-integrated applications such as Workspace with Gemini. The attacker plants adversarial natural-language instructions inside any external data the model ingests while servicing a user query — emails, documents, calendar invites, web pages, screenshots, or tool/connector outputs. When the LLM reads that untrusted content as part of its context, the embedded instructions override or augment the system/user prompt, causing the model to follow the attacker's intent instead of (or in addition to) the legitimate user request. IPI does not require any input directly from the victim user and survives the application boundary. Demonstrated downstream effects include bypassing Gemini privacy/safety controls (Miggo calendar-invite attack, Jan 19 2026), generating convincing phishing content inside Gemini for Workspace (HiddenLayer, Sep 25 2024), exfiltrating data through tool calls (Unit 42 AI-agent telemetry), and executing tool actions on the victim's authenticated session in agentic AI workflows.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Workspace with Gemini (Gemini app, plus Gemini features in Gmail, Docs, Drive, and Chat); Gemini 2.5 models
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes — multiple public proof-of-concept and weaponized examples exist for indirect prompt injection (applicable to Workspace/Gemini-style agentic LLM apps): https://github.com/brennanbrown/atlas-prompt-injection-poc (Atlas/Comet AI browser PoC HTML); https://github.com/swisskyrepo/PayloadsAllTheThings/blob/master/Prompt%20Injection/README.md (curated prompt-injection payload library); https://blog.trailofbits.com/2025/08/06/prompt-injection-engineering-for-attackers-exploiting-github-copilot/ (Trail of Bits Copilot IPI); https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/ai-agent-prompt-injection/ (live in-the-wild URL reviewerpress[.]com); https://www.forcepoint.com/blog/x-labs/indirect-prompt-injection-payloads (10 verified live-website IPI payloads); https://immersivelabs.com (PoC bypassing email security via IPI).
enrichment.patch_available: No traditional CVE-scored patch is published for this threat class; Google applies continuously deployed hardening instead. The canonical vendor communications are: (1) https://blog.google/security/google-workspaces-continuous-approach-to-mitigating-indirect-prompt-injections/ and (2) https://blog.google/security/mitigating-prompt-injection-attacks/ (companion technical explanation with Gemini 2.5 model-level mitigations, Jun 13 2025). Workspace administrator guidance is at https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/security/indirect-prompt-injections-and-googles-layered-defense-strategy-for-gemini.
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes — confirmed in-the-wild exploitation of the IPI threat class is documented across multiple authoritative security researchers, though no specific threat actor group has been publicly named for Workspace/Gemini IPI exploitation: (1) Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 — 'Fooling AI Agents: Web-Based Indirect Prompt Injection Observed in the Wild' (Mar 3 2026) states IPI 'is no longer merely theoretical but is being actively weaponized' based on large-scale telemetry; (2) Forcepoint X-Labs — '10 Indirect Prompt Injection Payloads Caught in the Wild' (Apr 22 2026) cataloged 10 verified live-website IPI payloads spanning financial fraud and data destruction; (3) Proofpoint — 'Cybersecurity stop of the month: how threat actors weaponize AI assistants with indirect prompt injection' (Nov 18 2025); (4) Immersive Labs published a PoC bypassing enterprise email-security products via IPI in HTML (Jul 21 2025).
enrichment.mitigation: Google's layered mitigation strategy for Workspace with Gemini: (a) prompt-injection content classifiers on ingested data; (b) security-thought reinforcement during model training to improve internal rejection of injected directives; (c) markdown sanitization and suspicious-URL redaction before the model acts on rendered output; (d) a user-confirmation framework that requires explicit user approval before sensitive tool calls; (e) end-user security-mitigation notifications surfacing risky model behavior; (f) model resilience/adversarial training that significantly hardened Gemini 2.5 models. Deterministic platform controls additionally include URL sanitization and tool-chaining policies. Google recommends keeping Workspace with Gemini on the latest model rollout and enabling all available end-user security notifications.

findings.title: Architecting Security for Agentic Capabilities in Chrome
findings.summary: Posted by Nathan Parker, Chrome security team Chrome has been advancing the web’s security for well over 15 years, and we’re committed to meeting new challenges and opportunities with AI. Billions of people trust Chrome to keep them safe by default, and this is a responsibility we take seriously. Following the recent launch of Gemini in Chrome and the preview of agentic capabilities , we want to share our approach and some new innovations to improve the safety of agentic browsing. The primary new threat facing all agentic browsers is indirect prompt injection . It can appear in malicious sites, third-party content in iframes, or from user-generated content like user reviews, and can cause the agent to take unwanted actions such as initiating financial transactions or exfiltrating sensitive data. Given this open challenge, we are investing in a layered defense that includes both deterministic and probabilistic defenses to make it difficult and costly for attackers to cause harm. Designing safe agentic browsing for Chrome has involved deep collaboration of security experts across Google. We built on Gemini's existing protections and agent security principles and have implemented several new layers for Chrome. We’re introducing a user alignment critic where the agent’s actions are vetted by a separate model that is isolated from untrusted content. We’re also extending Chrome’s origin-isolation capabilities to constrain what origins the agent can interact with, to just those that are relevant to the task. Our layered defense also includes user confirmations for critical steps, real-time detection of threats , and red-teaming and response . We’ll step through these layers below. Checking agent outputs with User Alignment Critic The main planning model for Gemini uses page content shared in Chrome to decide what action to take next. Exposure to untrusted web content means it is inherently vulnerable to indirect prompt injection. We use techniques like spotlighting that di

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect prompt injection (IDPI) is the primary new threat facing agentic browsers. An attacker embeds hidden or disguised instructions into web content that the Gemini in Chrome agent will ingest as part of its browsing task (e.g., as user-generated content such as product reviews, third-party iframes, or content the user asked the agent to read). Common delivery techniques include CSS-based hiding (display:none, visibility:hidden, opacity:0, white-on-white camouflage, font-size:0, off-screen positioning), HTML attribute cloaking, XML/SVG CDATA encapsulation, and Base64-encoded runtime assembly with timed delays. When the page content reaches the Gemini planning model, the hidden instructions are interpreted as legitimate commands, causing the agent to take attacker-controlled actions such as exfiltrating sensitive page data (form fields, tokens, account page contents), performing unauthorized transactions (e.g., banking actions), accessing local files or credentials, manipulating browser state, or accepting phishing instructions. Because the agent has both read access to the page DOM and the ability to take privileged actions on behalf of the user, IDPI bypasses traditional sandboxing that assumes user instructions and page content are trustworthy.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Chrome with the Gemini in Chrome / agentic browsing integration. Gemini in Chrome began rolling out to all Mac and Windows desktop users in the United States on 2025-09-18, and the 'Auto Browse' agentic capability is available to Google AI Pro and AI Ultra subscribers in the United States. The blog post does not specify a numeric Chrome version cut-off; the architectural defenses described apply to the rolling-out / upcoming agentic capabilities in Chrome.
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes. Multiple public PoCs demonstrate indirect prompt injection attacks against agentic AI browsers and Gemini products. Key PoCs include: (1) University of Washington / Roesner & Kohlbrenner 'Agentic Browsers and the Same-Origin Policy' (https://agent-security.cs.washington.edu/agentic_browsers_sop.html, Apr 15, 2026) — cross-origin data-theft PoC tested across Chrome with Gemini, Atlas, Comet, Claude for Chrome, Edge Copilot, etc.; (2) Brave's 'Indirect Prompt Injection in Perplexity Comet' PoC (https://brave.com/blog/comet-prompt-injection/, Aug 20, 2025); (3) 0din's 'Phishing for Gemini' PoC against Gemini for Workspace (https://0din.ai/blog/phishing-for-gemini, Jul 10, 2025); (4) Cyera Research Labs' prompt-injection PoC against Gemini CLI (https://www.cyera.com/research/cyera-research-labs-discloses-command-prompt-injection-vulnerabilities-in-gemini-cli, Nov 17, 2025); (5) Miggo's 'Weaponizing Calendar Invites: A Semantic Attack on Google Gemini' (https://www.miggo.io/post/weaponizing-calendar-invites-a-semantic-attack-on-google-gemini, Jan 19, 2026); (6) Tenable TRA-2025-23 on Gemini Search Personalization prompt injection (https://www.tenable.com/security/research/tra-2025-23, Jul 25, 2025); (7) related Palo Alto Unit 42 PoC on CVE-2026-0628 Chrome Gemini Live hijack (https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/gemini-live-in-chrome-hijacking/, Mar 2, 2026). No PoC URL is published by Google itself for the defenses described in the Dec 8, 2025 blog post.
enrichment.patch_available: Google's advisory describes progressively deployed architectural defenses (User Alignment Critic, Agent Origin Sets, prompt-injection classifier, deterministic user confirmations) being rolled out as part of Gemini in Chrome's agentic-capabilities preview rather than a discrete patch keyed to a specific Chrome build. No patch-version or CVE number is cited in the Dec 8, 2025 blog post. Authoritative advisory: https://blog.google/security/architecting-security-for-agentic/
enrichment.active_exploitation: Indirect prompt injection has been observed in the wild against AI agents more broadly — Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 documented observed web-based IDPI attacks in 'Fooling AI Agents: Web-Based Indirect Prompt Injection Observed in the Wild' (Mar 3, 2026: https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/ai-agent-prompt-injection/). However, no specific in-the-wild exploitation of the Chrome Gemini agentic-browsing surface is tied directly to this Dec 8, 2025 advisory. Multiple researcher-PoC demonstrations (Brave, UW, 0din, Cyera, Miggo, Tenable, Unit 42) confirm the threat is technically feasible against agentic browser targets, and a related Chrome-specific local-file-access flaw (CVE-2026-0628 in Gemini Live, patched) was demonstrated via malicious extensions. No confirmed in-the-wild campaign exploiting the Chrome-side Gemini panel under this specific advisory has been reported.
enrichment.threat_actors: None known. The Dec 8, 2025 Google Security Blog advisory does not attribute exploitation of indirect prompt injection against Chrome's Gemini agentic capabilities to any specific APT or threat actor group. Researchers (Palo Alto Unit 42, University of Washington, Brave, 0din.ai, Cyera, Miggo, Tenable) have published relevant proofs-of-concept, but no known in-the-wild named threat actor campaign has been tied to this specific advisory.
enrichment.mitigation: Google's announced defense-in-depth architecture for Gemini in Chrome agentic browsing (per the Dec 8, 2025 advisory and corroborating coverage) comprises: (1) User Alignment Critic — a separate, isolated Gemini model that vets proposed agent actions against the user's original intent using metadata only (no exposure to untrusted web content), acting as a 'high-trust system component'; (2) Agent Origin Sets (origin isolation) — categorizes web origins into 'read-only' (data may be consumed) and 'read-writable' (data may be sent/form-submitted to), with strict no-cross-isolation gating to bound cross-origin data leakage; (3) deterministic user confirmations — required for sensitive actions (banking/financial sites, medical sites, Google Password Manager sign-ins) and for first-encountered origins; (4) a real-time prompt-injection classifier running in parallel with the planning model's inference to block unaligned actions; (5) continuous automated and human red-teaming to discover new IDPI variants; (6) Spotlighting research (arXiv 2403.14720) combined with Chrome's existing Site Isolation and same-origin policy. User hardening advice (per Google's regular Chrome guidance) is to keep Chrome fully updated, enable Enhanced Safe Browsing / Google Password Manager, and avoid installing untrusted extensions that can interact with the Gemini panel.

findings.title: Rust in Android: move fast and fix things
findings.summary: Posted by Jeff Vander Stoep, Android Last year, we wrote about why a memory safety strategy that focuses on vulnerability prevention in new code quickly yields durable and compounding gains. This year we look at how this approach isn’t just fixing things, but helping us move faster . The 2025 data continues to validate the approach, with memory safety vulnerabilities falling below 20% of total vulnerabilities for the first time. Updated data for 2025. This data covers first-party and third-party (open source) code changes to the Android platform across C, C++, Java, Kotlin, and Rust. This post is published a couple of months before the end of 2025, but Android’s industry-standard 90-day patch window means that these results are very likely close to final. We can and will accelerate patching when necessary. We adopted Rust for its security and are seeing a 1000x reduction in memory safety vulnerability density compared to Android’s C and C++ code . But the biggest surprise was Rust's impact on software delivery. With Rust changes having a 4x lower rollback rate and spending 25% less time in code review , the safer path is now also the faster one. In this post, we dig into the data behind this shift and also cover: How we’re expanding our reach: We're pushing to make secure code the default across our entire software stack. We have updates on Rust adoption in first-party apps, the Linux kernel, and firmware. Our first rust memory safety vulnerability...almost: We'll analyze a near-miss memory safety bug in unsafe Rust: how it happened, how it was mitigated, and steps we're taking to prevent recurrence. It’s also a good chance to answer the question “if Rust can have memory safety issues, why bother at all?” Building Better Software, Faster Developing an operating system requires the low-level control and predictability of systems programming languages like C, C++, and Rust. While Java and Kotlin are important for Android platform development, their role is complementa

enrichment.technical_details: An incorrect bounds-check flaw in the Android CrabbyAVIF AVIF parser/decoder led to out-of-bounds read/write accesses (CWE-125) via a linear buffer overflow in the component's 'unsafe' Rust code path. A specially crafted AVIF image processed by CrabbyAVIF triggers the OOB access, potentially enabling remote code execution. Because the bug was caught by Google's tests before any public Android release shipped the affected code, the Scudo hardened allocator (with guard pages around secondary allocations) would have rendered it non-exploitable in practice.
enrichment.affected_products: Android AOSP 16 (platform/external/rust/crabbyavif package — CrabbyAVIF AVIF parser/decoder)
enrichment.exploit_available: No public exploit or proof-of-concept code is available. The vulnerability was an internal near-miss discovered before being shipped in any public Android release.
enrichment.patch_available: Yes — fixed in the Android Security Bulletin – August 2025 (reference A-419563680). Reference commits: https://android.googlesource.com/platform/external/rust/crabbyavif/+/9bcc1a311114ab844b417d3cdec50dcedfd0603f and https://android.googlesource.com/platform/external/rust/crabbyavif/+/42feb427a42afd9eebe6391a0d8a7b083fe82140. Official advisory: https://source.android.com/docs/security/bulletin/2025-08-01.
enrichment.active_exploitation: No. The vulnerability was caught by Google's internal testing before being shipped in any public Android release, so no in-the-wild exploitation has been observed. SentinelOne confirms no active exploitation. The CVE-2025-48530 entry was assigned primarily to track the patch and lessons learned through release channels.
enrichment.mitigation: Apply Android Security Patch Level 2025-08-01 or later to install the fixed CrabbyAVIF package. The Scudo hardened allocator with guard pages on secondary allocations serves as defense-in-depth by limiting the exploitability of out-of-bounds writes. Google has also expanded its Comprehensive Rust training with an unsafe-code deep dive covering soundness, UB reasoning, safety comments, and encapsulation to prevent recurrence.

findings.title: Mitigating prompt injection attacks with a layered defense strategy
findings.summary: Posted by Adam Gavish, Google GenAI Security Team With the rapid adoption of generative AI, a new wave of threats is emerging across the industry with the aim of manipulating the AI systems themselves. One such emerging attack vector is indirect prompt injections. Unlike direct prompt injections, where an attacker directly inputs malicious commands into a prompt, indirect prompt injections involve hidden malicious instructions within external data sources. These may include emails, documents, or calendar invites that instruct AI to exfiltrate user data or execute other rogue actions. As more governments, businesses, and individuals adopt generative AI to get more done, this subtle yet potentially potent attack becomes increasingly pertinent across the industry, demanding immediate attention and robust security measures. At Google, our teams have a longstanding precedent of investing in a defense-in-depth strategy, including robust evaluation, threat analysis, AI security best practices , AI red-teaming , adversarial training, and model hardening for generative AI tools. This approach enables safer adoption of Gemini in Google Workspace and the Gemini app (we refer to both in this blog as “Gemini” for simplicity). Below we describe our prompt injection mitigation product strategy based on extensive research, development, and deployment of improved security mitigations. A layered security approach Google has taken a layered security approach introducing security measures designed for each stage of the prompt lifecycle. From Gemini 2.5 model hardening, to purpose-built machine learning (ML) models detecting malicious instructions, to system-level safeguards, we are meaningfully elevating the difficulty, expense, and complexity faced by an attacker. This approach compels adversaries to resort to methods that are either more easily identified or demand greater resources. Our model training with adversarial data significantly enhanced our defenses against indirect prompt

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect prompt injection (IPI) is an attack class against LLM-driven products in which an attacker embeds hidden malicious natural-language instructions inside untrusted external content that the model later ingests (emails, documents, calendar invites, web pages). When the user subsequently issues a benign query, the LLM conflates data and instructions and is steered to perform attacker-chosen actions such as exfiltrating the user's private context (summaries, calendar data, mail contents) through outbound tool calls (e.g., Calendar.create, Send email, render image with attacker-controlled URL) that the user has authorised. Unlike direct prompt injection, IPI requires no direct attacker access to the prompt and no user interaction beyond receiving the booby-trapped document. Google's post notes that Gemini 2.5 model training with adversarial data and the full layered stack significantly enhanced defenses against indirect prompt injection attacks in Gemini 2.5 models.
enrichment.affected_products: Gemini app; Gemini in Google Workspace (Gmail, Docs editors, Drive, Chat); Gemini 2.5 family of models
enrichment.exploit_available: No proof-of-concept exploit is referenced in the original Google blog. Multiple research PoCs exist for the broader prompt injection class: (1) CVE-2025-32711 'EchoLeak' against M365 Copilot — explicitly NOT applicable to Gemini per Google; (2) Miggo calendar invite attack against Google Gemini (https://www.miggo.io/post/weaponizing-calendar-invites-a-semantic-attack-on-google-gemini); (3) HiddenLayer Gemini for Workspace PoC (https://www.hiddenlayer.com/research/new-gemini-for-workspace-vulnerability-enabling-phishing-content-manipulation); (4) Zenity ChatGPT connector PoC for Google Drive/SharePoint.
enrichment.patch_available: No traditional single patch. Google is rolling out proprietary ML classifiers to detect malicious prompts and is working to enhance inherent resilience in upcoming Gemini models ('later this year' per the June 13, 2025 post). Updates are delivered as continuous product/model updates rather than a discrete CVE fix. Advisory URLs: https://blog.google/security/mitigating-prompt-injection-attacks/ (canonical) and https://blog.google/security/google-workspaces-continuous-approach-to-mitigating-indirect-prompt-injections/ (Apr 2, 2026 follow-up).
enrichment.active_exploitation: The original Google blog does NOT report confirmed in-the-wild exploitation of prompt injection against Gemini; it frames indirect prompt injection as an 'emerging attack vector' and industry-wide concern. However, confirmed real-world exploitation reports against Google and competing GenAI products include: (a) Miggo disclosed (Jan 19, 2026) a Google Gemini calendar-invite IPI enabling exfiltration of private meeting data via Calendar.create — PoC worked end-to-end; (b) HiddenLayer disclosed (Sep 25, 2024) a Google Gemini for Workspace vulnerability enabling phishing content manipulation; (c) Aim Security / MSRC disclosed CVE-2025-32711 'EchoLeak' zero-click exfiltration against M365 Copilot (Google notes not applicable to Gemini); (d) Zenity publicly disclosed (Aug 6, 2025) a prompt-injection chain against ChatGPT's Google Drive/SharePoint connector; (e) Palo Alto Unit 42 observed in-the-wild web-based indirect prompt injection against AI agents (Mar 3, 2026). No specific APT group or ransomware-actor campaign has been publicly attributed to this vulnerability class.
enrichment.mitigation: Google's layered defense strategy for Gemini app and Gemini in Workspace comprises six components: (1) Model hardening — Gemini 2.5 was retrained with adversarial IPI data; (2) Prompt-injection content classifiers — proprietary ML models detecting malicious prompts in emails, files, and data formats; (3) Security thought reinforcement — system-level security instructions telling the LLM to follow user intent and ignore adversarial instructions; (4) Markdown sanitization and suspicious-URL redaction — external image URLs are identified and not rendered, and URLs are screened against Google Safe Browsing; (5) User confirmation framework (HITL) — sensitive actions (e.g., deleting a calendar event) require explicit user confirmation; (6) End-user security mitigation notifications — contextual warnings plus help-center links when mitigations fire. Programmatic measures: manual and automated red-teaming, GenAI security BugSWAT events, the Secure AI Framework (SAIF), the AI Vulnerability Reward Program (AI VRP), and Coalition for Secure AI (CoSAI) partnership. Workspace admin guidance at https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/security/indirect-prompt-injections-and-googles-layered-defense-strategy-for-gemini.

findings.title: Countering Chinese State-Sponsored Actors Compromise of Networks Worldwide to Feed Global Espionage System
findings.summary: Executive summary People’s Republic of China (PRC) state-sponsored cyber threat actors are targeting networks globally, including, but not limited to, telecommunications, government, transportation, lodging, and military infrastructure networks. While these actors focus on large backbone routers of major telecommunications providers, as well as provider edge (PE) and customer edge (CE) routers, they also leverage compromised devices and trusted connections to pivot into other networks. These actors often modify routers to maintain persistent, long-term access to networks. This activity partially overlaps with cyber threat actor reporting by the cybersecurity industry—commonly referred to as Salt Typhoon, OPERATOR PANDA, RedMike, UNC5807, and GhostEmperor, among others. The authoring agencies are not adopting a particular commercial naming convention and hereafter refer to those responsible for the cyber threat activity more generically as “Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) actors” throughout this advisory. This cluster of cyber threat activity has been observed in the United States, Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the United Kingdom, and other areas globally. This Cybersecurity Advisory (CSA) includes observations from various government and industry investigations where the APT actors targeted internal enterprise environments, as well as systems and networks that deliver services directly to customers. This CSA details the tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) leveraged by these APT actors to facilitate detection and threat hunting, and provides mitigation guidance to reduce the risk from these APT actors and their TTPs. This CSA is being released by the following authoring and co-sealing agencies: United States National Security Agency (NSA) United States Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) United States Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) United States Department of Defense Cyber Crime Center (DC3) Australian Signals Directorate’s Austra

enrichment.technical_details: AA25-239A details PRC APT exploitation of unpatched internet-facing edge devices via chained vulnerabilities: Cisco IOS XE Web UI auth bypass (CVE-2023-20198) chained with privilege escalation (CVE-2023-20273) to write local user accounts; Cisco Smart Install RCE (CVE-2018-0171); Ivanti Connect Secure auth bypass (CVE-2023-46805) chained with command injection (CVE-2024-21887); Palo Alto GlobalProtect command injection (CVE-2024-3400). Persistence techniques include: (1) ACL modification (often named 'access-list 10', '20', '50') to permit actor-controlled IPs, opening high ports, inserting SSH authorized-keys backdoors; (2) TACACS+/AAA/RADIUS tampering to harvest credentials; (3) local privileged account creation and Type 5/7 hash brute-forcing; (4) hiding in Cisco IOS XE/NX-OS Guest Shell containers to evade syslog; (5) GRE/IPsec tunnel exfiltration. Lateral movement and reconnaissance: harvesting router configs, BGP/MPLS/MIB data, and SPAN/RSPAN/ERSPAN mirroring. Evasion: local log clearing and disabling of log forwarding.
enrichment.affected_products: Cisco IOS and IOS XE Software (including Smart Install client feature and Web UI feature on IOS XE 16.x+); Ivanti Connect Secure 9.x and 22.x; Ivanti Policy Secure 9.x and 22.x; Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS with GlobalProtect enabled (PAN-OS 11.1.x < 11.1.2-h3, 11.0.x < 11.0.4-h1, 10.2.x < 10.2.9-h1). Additional edge-device vendors (Fortinet, Juniper, Microsoft Exchange) are noted by CISA as potentially leveraged but specific vulnerable versions are not enumerated in the advisory.
enrichment.cvss_vector: CVSS v3.x vectors for the six CVEs cited in AA25-239A Appendix B: CVE-2023-20198 = CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H (10.0); CVE-2018-0171 = CVSS:3.0/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H (9.8); CVE-2023-20273 = CVSS:3.1/AV:L/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H (7.2); CVE-2024-21887 = CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H (9.1); CVE-2023-46805 = CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H (8.2); CVE-2024-3400 = CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H (10.0; CVSS v4.0 also 10.0).
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes. Public exploits/PoCs exist for all six CVEs in Appendix B of AA25-239A, and most appear in the CISA KEV catalog. CVE-2023-20198/CVE-2023-20273 (Cisco IOS XE Web UI) were mass-exploited by Salt Typhoon/RedMike in 2023-2024. CVE-2024-21887/CVE-2023-46805 (Ivanti) have public PoCs. CVE-2024-3400 (PAN-OS GlobalProtect) was exploited as a zero-day upon disclosure. CVE-2018-0171 (Cisco Smart Install) has public exploit code and is in the KEV catalog.
enrichment.patch_available: Yes - vendor patches are available for all six CVEs in AA25-239A Appendix B. Cisco: CVE-2018-0171 (https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/support/docs/csa/cisco-sa-20180328-smi2.html) and CVE-2023-20198/CVE-2023-20273 (https://sec.cloudapps.cisco.com/security/center/content/CiscoSecurityAdvisory/cisco-sa-iosxe-webui-privesc-j22SaA4z); Ivanti: CVE-2023-46805/CVE-2024-21887 (https://hub.ivanti.com/s/article/KB-CVE-2023-46805-Authentication-Bypass-CVE-2024-21887-Command-Injection-for-Ivanti-Connect-Secure-and-Ivanti-Policy-Secure-Gateways); Palo Alto Networks: CVE-2024-3400 (https://security.paloaltonetworks.com/CVE-2024-3400, fixed in PAN-OS 11.1.0-h3, 11.0.1-h4, 10.2.4-h16, plus later hotfixes).
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes - confirmed active exploitation in the wild. CISA AA25-239A documents ongoing PRC APT operations targeting telecommunications carriers (Salt Typhoon/OPERATOR PANDA), enterprise and government networks globally (US, AU, CA, NZ, UK), using the six CVEs in Appendix B as initial access. Salt Typhoon campaign publicly reported by CISA/NSA/FBI/ACSC/CCCS/NCSC-NZ starting August 2025; targets include telecommunications, government, transportation, lodging, and military infrastructure. Per CISA, 'exploitation of zero-day vulnerabilities has not been observed to date' — all listed CVEs are n-day with available patches, not zero-days.
enrichment.threat_actors: PRC state-sponsored APT groups: Salt Typhoon (aka OPERATOR PANDA, FamousSparrow, Earth Estries, GhostEmperor, UNC5807), RedMike, and GhostEmperor. Salt Typhoon is attributed to China's Ministry of State Security (MSS) and is responsible for the majority of telecom-provider compromises; RedMike targets telecom providers via Cisco IOS XE CVEs; GhostEmperor/UNC5807 are additional PRC clusters in the same campaign activity.
enrichment.mitigation: Apply vendor patches for all six CVEs in Appendix B; disable Cisco Smart Install (SMI) where not required; disable or restrict the IOS XE Web UI on edge devices; restrict the Palo Alto GlobalProtect interface to internal/management networks; move management services off the public internet behind MFA + jump hosts; pull full device configurations periodically and diff against authorized golden config; audit ACLs for unauthorized additions (especially '10', '20', '50') and remote-access/tunneling entries; audit TACACS+/AAA/RADIUS server IPs and settings; rotate all credentials and decommission weak Type 5/7 hashes; monitor for unexpected GRE/IPsec tunnels and SPAN/RSPAN/ERSPAN mirroring; monitor for packet captures and unauthorized Guest Shell containers; enforce robust change-management with periodic configuration audits; if previously compromised, fully rebuild and reimage (factory reset is insufficient for Guest Shell implants or persisted configuration changes).

findings.title: Russian GRU Targeting Western Logistics Entities and Technology Companies
findings.summary: Executive Summary This joint cybersecurity advisory (CSA) highlights a Russian state-sponsored cyber campaign targeting Western logistics entities and technology companies. This includes those involved in the coordination, transport, and delivery of foreign assistance to Ukraine. Since 2022, Western logistics entities and IT companies have faced an elevated risk of targeting by the Russian General Staff Main Intelligence Directorate (GRU) 85th Main Special Service Center (85th GTsSS), military unit 26165—tracked in the cybersecurity community under several names (see “ Cybersecurity Industry Tracking ”). The actors’ cyber espionage-oriented campaign, targeting technology companies and logistics entities, uses a mix of previously disclosed tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs). The authoring agencies expect similar targeting and TTP use to continue. Executives and network defenders at logistics entities and technology companies should recognize the elevated threat of unit 26165 targeting, increase monitoring and threat hunting for known TTPs and indicators of compromise (IOCs), and posture network defenses with a presumption of targeting. This cyber espionage-oriented campaign targeting logistics entities and technology companies uses a mix of previously disclosed TTPs and is likely connected to these actors’ wide scale targeting of IP cameras in Ukraine and bordering NATO nations. The following authors and co-sealers are releasing this CSA: United States National Security Agency (NSA) United States Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) United Kingdom National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC-UK) Germany Federal Intelligence Service (BND) Bundesnachrichtendienst Germany Federal Office for Information Security (BSI) Bundesamt für Sicherheit in der Informationstechnik Germany Federal Office for the Protection of the Constitution (BfV) Bundesamt für Verfassungsschutz Czech Republic Military Intelligence (VZ) Vojenské zpravodajství Czech Republic National Cyber and Infor

enrichment.technical_details: GRU Unit 26165 conducts extensive reconnaissance (T1589/T1591/T1592) to map victim supply chains and trust relationships, then gains initial access via six parallel techniques: (1) spearphishing with spoofed login pages and MFA/CAPTCHA bypass (T1566), (2) credential guessing/brute force against Internet-facing accounts from Tor/commercial-VPN infrastructure (T1110.001/T1110.003), (3) exploitation of unpatched Internet-facing apps and corporate VPNs (T1133), (4) SQL injection (T1190), and (5) exploitation of trusted third-party / supply-chain relationships (T1199). Exploited CVEs include CVE-2023-23397 (Outlook NTLM hash leak via PidLidReminderFileParameter), CVE-2023-38831 (WinRAR ZIP/RCE), and CVE-2021-44026 (Roundcube SQLi). Execution leverages PowerShell (including clipboard-hidden commands), DLL search-order hijacking (T1574.001), and HTA/shortcut/scheduled-task abuse. Persistence via scheduled tasks (T1053.005), Run/RunOnce keys, and Startup folder shortcuts (T1547). Lateral movement via RDP, PsExec, Impacket (atexec, smbexec, wmiexec), and harvested RDP pivots. Credential theft via ntdsutil AD-database dumping, Get-GPPPassword.ps1 GPP XML decryption, Certipy ADCS enumeration/exploitation, and password spraying. Defense evasion via wevtutil cl (T1070.001), intermittent encryption, and Tor-fronted C2. Exfiltration via staged .zip + OpenSSH/Dropbox to cloud steal endpoints. Parallel tradecraft leverages compromised IP cameras (RTSP DESCRIBE brute force with default/guessed creds) for surveillance and SOHO devices abused as UPnP/P2P TLS proxies (T1665). Full IOC infrastructure is published in the AA25-141A STIX bundle.
enrichment.affected_products: AA25-141A is a threat-activity/TTP advisory, not a single-CVE bulletin, but it documents active GRU Unit 26165 exploitation of: Microsoft Outlook for Windows (CVE-2023-23397 EoP, patched March 14 2023); RARLAB WinRAR < 6.23 (CVE-2023-38831 ZIP archive RCE, patched August 2 2023); Roundcube Webmail < 1.3.17 and 1.4.x < 1.4.12 (CVE-2021-44026 SQL injection, plus referenced CVE-2020-12641 and CVE-2020-35730 XSS); Microsoft Exchange (spearphishing pretexting); Internet-connected IP cameras / RTSP-enabled surveillance devices; small-office/home-office (SOHO) routers and network-attached devices coerced into proxying; corporate VPNs and other Internet-facing authentication infrastructure; and ICS components in Western rail/air/maritime logistics. Targeted sectors: defense industrial base, transportation (rail, air, ports, maritime), IT services, and entities coordinating foreign aid deliveries to Ukraine.
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes. Public PoC / weaponized exploit code exists for all underlying CVEs Unit 26165 leveraged: CVE-2023-23397 (Microsoft detection script at https://aka.ms/CVE-2023-23397ScriptDoc and Metasploit module), CVE-2023-38831 (publicly documented as a zero-day exploited in the wild before patching by Group-IB/McAfee, public PoC code released), and CVE-2021-44026 (public SQLi exploit code for Roundcube < 1.3.17 / < 1.4.12). Unit 26165 additionally weaponized custom malware families — HEADLACE, MASEPIE, OCEANMAP, and STEELHOOK — plus open-source tooling (Impacket, PsExec, Certipy, Get-GPPPassword, ntdsutil, vssadmin, wevtutil, Metasploit), forming a fully weaponized kill chain. YARA rules available at https://github.com/Neo23x0/signature-base/blob/master/yara/gen_impacket_tools.yar. Corroborating analyses: https://attackiq.com/2025/05/21/response-to-cisa-advisory-aa25-141a and https://www.safebreach.com/blog/safebreach-coverage-for-us-cert-aa25-141a-russian-gru-espionage.
enrichment.patch_available: Yes — official vendor patches exist for every CVE Unit 26165 leveraged: (1) Microsoft Outlook — patched March 14 2023 for CVE-2023-23397, see https://msrc.microsoft.com/update-guide/vulnerability/CVE-2023-23397 with detection script at https://aka.ms/CVE-2023-23397ScriptDoc; (2) RARLAB WinRAR — fixed in WinRAR 6.23 on August 2 2023 for CVE-2023-38831 (download via https://www.rarlab.com/); (3) Roundcube Webmail — fixed in Roundcube 1.3.17 and 1.4.12 for CVE-2021-44026; (4) Microsoft Exchange — monthly security updates per Microsoft release cadence. Full advisory at https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-141a (last revised April 17 2026). The CTI advisory itself directs organizations to prioritize patching these CVEs first.
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes — confirmed active in-the-wild exploitation. CISA et al. describe a sustained two-plus-year GRU Unit 26165 cyber-espionage campaign that has been ongoing since 2022 against Western logistics entities and technology companies (including those coordinating foreign assistance to Ukraine). The advisory documents numerous confirmed compromises across defense, transport (rail/air/maritime/ports), IT/services, and aid-coordination victim organizations. Sources confirming active exploitation: the joint CISA/FBI/NSA/ACSC/CCCS/NCSC-NZ advisory AA25-141A (https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-141a, published May 21 2025, last revised April 17 2026); CISA AA25-141A STIX IOC bundle; corroborating analyses including SafeBreach (https://www.safebreach.com/blog/safebreach-coverage-for-us-cert-aa25-141a-russian-gru-espionage), SOC Prime (https://socprime.com/blog/detect-apt28-attacks-against-western-companies-coordinating-aid-to-ukraine), and AttackIQ (https://attackiq.com/2025/05/21/response-to-cisa-advisory-aa25-141a).
enrichment.threat_actors: Russian General Staff Main Intelligence Directorate (GRU) 85th Main Special Service Center (85th GTsSS), military unit 26165. Industry-tracked aliases: APT28, Fancy Bear, Forest Blizzard, STRONTIUM, Sednit, Sofacy, BlueDelta, and UAC-0001 (per joint CISA/FBI/NSA/ACSC/CCCS/NCSC-NZ attribution).
enrichment.mitigation: Patch the exploited CVEs first, prioritizing: Microsoft Outlook (CVE-2023-23397) — apply the March 14 2023 Microsoft security update; RARLAB WinRAR ≥ 6.23 (CVE-2023-38831); Roundcube Webmail ≥ 1.3.17 or ≥ 1.4.12 (CVE-2021-44026); harden Microsoft Exchange and Outlook (disable NTLM where possible, enable ASR rules, SMB signing/LSA Protection). Network/identity hardening: enforce phishing-resistant MFA on all internet-facing accounts, audit and block anomalous NTLM/SMB traffic to external infrastructure (D3-OTF), adopt Zero Trust, deploy EDR with priority on mail servers and domain controllers, monitor Windows logs for unexpected log clearing, and audit critical-tool use (wevtutil, schtasks, ntdsutil, Certipy, vssadmin, Get-GPPPassword, PowerShell). Email/web hardening: enable attack-surface-reduction rules to block executable email content (D3-ABPI) and files in globally-writeable directories (D3-EAL); harden Roundcube/Exchange; detect credential-phishing landing pages and impersonated IT accounts. IP-camera / SOHO hardening: replace unsupported devices, apply firmware patches, disable UPnP/P2P/Anonymous Visit features, disable unused ports/services (FTP, web UI), enable authenticated RTSP only (D3-AA), restrict remote admin with VPN+MFA (D3-NI/D3-UBA), audit user accounts (D3-UAP), enable and centralize logging, and isolate cameras/SOHO devices from sensitive networks. Threat hunting: hunt HEADLACE/MASEPIE/OCEANMAP/STEELHOOK IOCs, Impacket artifacts (YARA: https://github.com/Neo23x0/signature-base/blob/master/yara/gen_impacket_tools.yar), ADCS / Certipy abuse, mailbox-permission manipulation, anonymized Tor/commercial-VPN session traffic, and SOHO device proxying. Full IOCs in AA25-141A STIX bundle on CISA.

findings.title: Browser Security: Zero-Days Are Only Part of the Problem

enrichment.technical_details: The blog describes the general browser attack model in narrative terms rather than documenting a specific exploit. Adversaries chain browser exploitation (e.g., flaws in rendering logic, JavaScript execution, document handling, or memory safety) with a follow-on sandbox escape or privilege escalation to move from browser activity to system-level access. They then layer phishing, OAuth/token abuse, malicious extensions, script-based credential capture, data exfiltration, malicious/compromised SaaS applications, or compromised AI tools to expand from initial browser foothold into broader enterprise compromise. No specific vulnerability mechanism is documented because the post addresses browser security thematically.
enrichment.affected_products: Chromium-based browsers (e.g., Google Chrome, Microsoft Edge) - no specific versions identified in the article; the blog refers generically to browsers and 'web technologies' built on the Chromium open-source core.
enrichment.exploit_available: The article does not publish or link to any specific proof-of-concept exploit. It states generally that for N-day browser vulnerabilities 'proof-of-concept code can become publicly available after disclosure, lowering the bar for opportunistic and financially motivated attackers.'
enrichment.patch_available: Patch availability for any specific vulnerability is unavailable (the article references no single CVE). The article does note browsers are 'increasingly delivered through auto-update mechanisms that are efficient at pushing security updates quickly' and recommends enterprises validate, test, stage, and confirm installation across managed and unmanaged devices.
enrichment.active_exploitation: The article reports general exploitation trends rather than a specific in-the-wild incident. Citing Verizon 2026 DBIR: 'vulnerability exploitation surpassed stolen credentials as the top breach entry point in 2025.' Citing the CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report: '42% of vulnerabilities were exploited before public disclosure.' It also notes generically that 'recently disclosed in-the-wild browser zero-days have reinforced what defenders already knew: the browser is one of the most exploited surfaces in modern enterprise environments.' No specific CVE or campaign is documented.
enrichment.mitigation: 1) Validate browser patch deployment: validate updates, test compatibility, stage deployment, and confirm installation across both managed and unmanaged devices. 2) Deploy browser-level runtime defenses such as CrowdStrike Falcon Secure Access, which uses JavaScript Language Randomization (JSLR) to continuously randomize the JavaScript runtime environment, blocks phishing and adversary-in-the-middle (AitM) techniques, protects session tokens against hijacking and MFA bypass, and prevents credential theft and data exfiltration. 3) Address unmanaged devices (personal smartphones, contractor laptops, BYOD endpoints) that may not be enrolled in enterprise patch management.

findings.summary: Noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: Anonymous-linked Canadian hacker jailed, researcher drops zero-days in open source projects, Venezuelans sentenced in the US over ATM jackpotting. The post In Other News: Canadian Hacker Jailed, Open Source Zero-Days, Two Sentenced for ATM Jackpotting appeared first on SecurityWeek .

findings.title: Bringing Rust to the Pixel Baseband
findings.summary: Posted by Jiacheng Lu, Software Engineer, Google Pixel Team Google is continuously advancing the security of Pixel devices. We have been focusing on hardening the cellular baseband modem against exploitation. Recognizing the risks associated within the complex modem firmware, Pixel 9 shipped with mitigations against a range of memory-safety vulnerabilities. For Pixel 10, Google is advancing its proactive security measures further. Following our previous discussion on "Deploying Rust in Existing Firmware Codebases" , this post shares a concrete application: integrating a memory-safe Rust DNS(Domain Name System) parser into the modem firmware. The new Rust-based DNS parser significantly reduces our security risk by mitigating an entire class of vulnerabilities in a risky area, while also laying the foundation for broader adoption of memory-safe code in other areas. Here we share our experience of working on it, and hope it can inspire the use of more memory safe languages in low-level environments. Why Modem Memory Safety Can’t Wait In recent years, we have seen increasing interest in the cellular modem from attackers and security researchers. For example, Google's Project Zero gained remote code execution on Pixel modems over the Internet. Pixel modem has tens of Megabytes of executable code. Given the complexity and remote attack surface of the modem, other critical memory safety vulnerabilities may remain in the predominantly memory-unsafe firmware code. Why DNS? The DNS protocol is most commonly known in the context of browsers finding websites. With the evolution of cellular technology, modern cellular communications have migrated to digital data networks; consequently, even basic operations such as call forwarding rely on DNS services. DNS is a complex protocol and requires parsing of untrusted data, which can lead to vulnerabilities, particularly when implemented in a memory-unsafe language (example: CVE-2024-27227 ). Implementing the DNS parser in Rust offers
